meta.title: Pirates and Privateers
meta.author: Mary Fifield
meta.focusQuestion: What were the real lives of pirates like, and how do they differ from the way pirates are shown in movies?

sections.title: Introduction
paragraphs: What do you think of when you think of pirates? Buried treasure? Ships with mighty sails? Black flags with a skull and crossbones? Have you ever wondered what it was like to be a pirate?

Pirates seemed to love adventure. They were often vicious men and women who were robbers and murderers. Pirates sometimes are glamorized in movies and books. But they were scoundrels, and the lives they lived were not that pleasant. Their lifestyles often resulted in great discomfort and even death.

sections.title: The "Golden Age" of Pirates
paragraphs: Throughout history, pirates have sailed the seas all over the world. From the 1500s to the 1700s, many famous pirates raided ships and ports in the Caribbean Sea. This was called the "Golden Age" of pirates.

During the "Golden Age," Spain colonized large parts of Central and South America. The Spaniards were very interested in the spices, gold, and silver found in the Americas. In order to get these treasures, they forced the native people to work in mines and to gather spices. They also stole gold and silver from the native people.

The English and French royalty learned that Spain was getting rich from gold, silver, and spices from the Americas. They wanted gold for their empires, too. At the same time, there were a lot of unemployed sailors. The sailors were looking for adventure and a chance to get rich.

The Queen of England developed a plan to get some of the wealth the Spanish were bringing across the Atlantic Ocean on their ships. Her plan called for hiring unemployed sailors and providing them with ships to attack the Spanish ships. In England, these sailors were called privateers. In Spain, they were called pirates.

Many of the ships leaving the Americas loaded with riches passed through the Caribbean Sea. Since the Caribbean was full of small islands, it was a perfect place for pirates to hang out. It was also a perfect place from which to launch attacks on Spanish ships passing among the islands.

sections.title: Pirates Attack!
paragraphs: Pirates quickly gained a reputation of being ruthless villains. Sailors on other ships were filled with fear when they saw a pirate ship approaching.
Most of the ships being attacked were cargo ships that lacked weapons for fighting off the pirates. The sailors knew there was little they could do to stop the pirates.

Some pirates used smaller and swifter boats for night attacks. They would come alongside a cargo ship in the darkness of night. Then they would quickly seize the ship and kill many of the unsuspecting crew.

Once on board, pirates used many kinds of weapons to take over a ship. One of the most common weapons was a cutlass. A cutlass is similar to a small sword. It was used for close-up, hand-to-hand fighting. Pirates also used guns, such as flintlock pistols and flintlock muskets.

Before they boarded a ship, pirates fired a warning shot from a cannon. They hoped that the captain and crew of the ship would simply give up their cargo without a fight. Cannon shots were also used to destroy masts and rigging on the other ship. Pirates then threw smoke bombs onto the ship's deck to confuse and frighten the crew. The pirates then used grappling hooks to climb aboard the ship.
Many people were wounded or killed in pirate battles. Sometimes, pirates would take hostages. They might even force members of a ship's crew to join the pirate crew.

It was the love of gold that caused most pirates—and the queens and kings who hired them—to steal. But pirates stole many other items of value. The ships that were attacked often carried silver and precious gems, such as emeralds and pearls. They also carried tobacco, wine, brandy, coffee, tea, spices, and expensive fabrics such as velvet and linen. Pirates sold or traded most of these goods on the black market.

sections.title: Life on a Pirate Ship
paragraphs: To some people, a pirate's life may seem exciting. But a pirate's life was not very glamorous. They were criminals who were hated as much as they were feared. They had to constantly be on guard and often couldn't even trust their fellow crew members. The work was hard and dangerous. Many pirates lost legs, arms, fingers, eyes, and even their lives in fierce battles.

Living conditions were not very pleasant, either. They often lived in dirty, smelly quarters. Pirates on a ship were assigned different jobs. One person was always assigned to stand watch. Another person, called a helmsman, steered the ship with a lever called a tiller.

The cooper was the person who made, repaired, and sealed all of the barrels on the ship. Most of the food and drink was stored in barrels. The cooper had to make sure the barrels were well-made and sealed. Otherwise, the food or drink might go bad.
There were other jobs, too, such as mending the sails, cooking for the crew, caulking the wooden deck planks, and pumping water from the bottom of the ship.

But when the work was done, pirates had time for eating, drinking, and entertainment. They ate meals together and drank liquor or wine. They played games such as dice or cards. They also played musical instruments, sang, and danced. Pirates were often lively and rowdy at mealtimes.

Pirates often stole clothes from people on other ships. The crew dressed differently than the captain. The captain usually wore much fancier clothing. Some captains liked to wear silk pants and shirts with lace cuffs and gold buttons.
Not all pirates were men. There were a few women pirates, as well. Most pirate ships did not allow women on board, so women pirates had to disguise themselves by dressing like men.

Many pirate ship crews had a voice in the running of their ship. They voted on rules and helped decide how the treasure from raids would be divided. Of course, the captain always got the biggest share.

sections.title: Well-Known Pirates
paragraphs: Many pirates became well known. And many stories of these pirates have been passed down over the years.
Blackbeard, whose real name was Edward Teach, was a notorious pirate born in England. He first served as a crew member on a pirate ship captained by a pirate named Benjamin Hornigold.

Blackbeard became one of the most feared pirate captains sailing the Caribbean. Rather than wait for his victims to take off their rings and give them to him, Blackbeard would cut their fingers off to get the rings.

What made Blackbeard even more terrifying was the way he looked in battle. He had a thick, black beard that came up to his eyes. Before a fight, he would tie ribbons in his beard and twist it into small pigtails. Then he would stick smoking flares into the pigtails so that his face would be surrounded by smoke.

Sir Francis Drake was a privateer for the Queen of England. The Queen hired him to attack Spanish ships and steal their gold and silver. Drake attacked Spanish ships and also attacked mule trains carrying gold and silver across land. He stole treasures for Queen Elizabeth.
Henry Morgan was another famous privateer. He was born in Wales and eventually became the lieutenant governor of Jamaica. Morgan attacked Spanish settlements and ships throughout the Caribbean.

sections.title: Conclusion
paragraphs: Pirates of today are quite different from the pirates of yesterday. Modern-day pirates attack ships on the seas, just as the pirates of yesterday did. But modern pirates use high-powered speedboats instead of sailing ships. They attack small boats that are out for pleasure along with bigger ships. They sell the valuables they steal on the black market.

Some people still find pirates exciting and glamorous. Movies and books continue to show pirates as daring adventurers who fight for freedom. But the real pirates of yesterday were robbers and murderers. Their lives were not exciting. They lived in dirty, crowded quarters and fought in bloody battles. Many lost their lives, and most did not live to be very old.

vocabulary.definition: an illegal system of buying and selling stolen goods
vocabulary.sentence: Pirates sold or traded most of these goods on the black market.

vocabulary.definition: sealing gaps with a waterproof material
vocabulary.sentence: There were other jobs, too, such as caulking the wooden deck planks.

vocabulary.definition: a person who makes and repairs barrels
vocabulary.sentence: The cooper was the person who made, repaired, and sealed all of the barrels on the ship.

vocabulary.definition: a short, curved sword used by sailors
vocabulary.sentence: One of the most common weapons was a cutlass. A cutlass is similar to a small sword.

vocabulary.definition: made to seem more attractive or exciting than it really is
vocabulary.sentence: Pirates sometimes are glamorized in movies and books.

options: made to seem more exciting than reality

vocabulary.definition: a hook attached to a rope, used for climbing aboard ships
vocabulary.sentence: The pirates then used grappling hooks to climb aboard the ship.

vocabulary.definition: a person who steers a ship
vocabulary.sentence: Another person, called a helmsman, steered the ship with a lever called a tiller.

options: the person who steers the ship

vocabulary.definition: to set in motion; to send out
vocabulary.sentence: It was also a perfect place from which to launch attacks on Spanish ships.

options: begin or send out the attack

vocabulary.definition: a woman employed to do domestic work
vocabulary.sentence: Some pirate captains kept a maidservant on board to attend to their needs.

vocabulary.definition: famous or well known for something bad
vocabulary.sentence: Blackbeard was a notorious pirate born in England.

vocabulary.definition: sailors hired by a government to attack enemy ships
vocabulary.sentence: In England, these sailors were called privateers. In Spain, they were called pirates.
Pirates were often lively and rowdy at mealtimes.

questions.question: Which of the following might pirates have done when they attacked a ship?
questions.evidence: Cannon shots were also used to destroy masts and rigging on the other ship. Pirates then threw smoke bombs onto the ship's deck. Sometimes, pirates would take hostages.
choices: Destroyed masts and rigging.
Thrown smoke bombs.
Taken hostages.
All of the above.
questions.explanation: Pirates did all of these things — they destroyed masts, threw smoke bombs, and took hostages during attacks.

questions.question: Why would the pirates fire a warning shot toward the ship they were ready to attack?
questions.evidence: They hoped that the captain and crew of the ship would simply give up their cargo without a fight.
choices: The other ship's crew would come on deck to fight.
The crew and captain might give up their cargo without a fight.
It would kill as many of the crew as they could.
All of the above.
questions.explanation: The warning shot was meant to scare the crew into surrendering without a fight.

questions.question: What is a cutlass?
questions.evidence: One of the most common weapons was a cutlass. A cutlass is similar to a small sword.
choices: A small sword.
A grenade.
A flintlock pistol.
A type of smoke bomb.
questions.explanation: A cutlass is a short, curved sword used for hand-to-hand fighting.

questions.question: Why did pirates have the reputation as ruthless villains?
questions.evidence: They attacked cargo ships that had no weapons. They used smaller boats for night attacks. They threw smoke bombs to frighten the crew.
choices: They attacked at night and killed many of the crew.
They threw smoke bombs to frighten the crew.
They attacked cargo ships that had no weapons.
All of the above.
questions.explanation: Pirates were ruthless because they attacked defenseless cargo ships — one of many reasons for their terrible reputation.

questions.question: Why did the pirates take so few of their own things onboard the ships?
questions.evidence: Like all sailors, pirates lived in close spaces with little privacy. They slept in hammocks. Their personal belongings were stored in sea chests.
choices: The captains would steal from them.
They were quite poor and did not own much.
They lived in very small spaces with little privacy.
They did not need many things.
questions.explanation: Pirates had small, crowded living spaces — hammocks in shared quarters — so they couldn't bring many personal belongings.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT something Sir Francis Drake did as a privateer?
questions.evidence: Drake attacked Spanish ships and also attacked mule trains carrying gold and silver across land. He stole treasures for Queen Elizabeth.
choices: Attacked mule trains.
Captained his own ship.
Stole treasures for Queen Elizabeth.
Limited his pirating activities to the sea.
questions.explanation: Drake did NOT limit his activities to the sea — he also attacked mule trains on land carrying gold and silver.

questions.question: Why did the captain wear fancier clothes than the crew?
questions.evidence: The captain usually wore much fancier clothing. Some captains liked to wear silk pants and shirts with lace cuffs and gold buttons.
choices: The crew didn't want to wear anything but simple clothes.
The captain didn't have to do physical work and wanted to look different.
The crew would try to steal the captain's clothes.
The captain stole the crew's nice clothes.
questions.explanation: Captains wore fancier clothes to set themselves apart — they were the leaders and wanted to look different from the crew.

questions.question: What did Blackbeard do that caused him to be so feared?
questions.evidence: Blackbeard would cut their fingers off to get their rings. He would tie ribbons in his beard and stick smoking flares into the pigtails. He fought fiercely.
choices: He would place smoking flares in his beard to scare people.
He would cut peoples' fingers off to get their rings.
All of the above.
He fought fiercely.
questions.explanation: All of these made Blackbeard terrifying — his fierce fighting, the smoking flares in his beard, and his cruel treatment of victims.

questions.question: Why would Queen Elizabeth NOT have considered Sir Francis Drake a pirate?
questions.evidence: The Queen of England developed a plan... for hiring unemployed sailors and providing them with ships to attack the Spanish ships. In England, these sailors were called privateers.
choices: He attacked Spanish ships and gave the treasures to Queen Elizabeth.
He didn't attack ships and steal gold and treasures.
He only attacked people on land at mines owned by others.
All of the above.
questions.explanation: Queen Elizabeth didn't consider Drake a pirate because he was her privateer — he attacked ENEMY ships and brought treasures to HER.

questions.question: What is a helmsman?
questions.evidence: Another person, called a helmsman, steered the ship with a lever called a tiller.
choices: A person who leads pirates onto a ship.
A woman who dresses as a pirate.
A person who steers a ship.
A person who mends sails.
questions.explanation: A helmsman is the person who steers the ship using a lever called a tiller.

questions.question: Explain two ways pirates today are different from pirates of yesterday. Use details from the text.
questions.explanation: Think about the tools, ships, and targets that modern pirates use compared to those of the Golden Age.
opinionPrompts: What kind of boats do modern pirates use?
What do they do with stolen goods?
How are pirates shown in movies vs. reality?

prompts.K: What do you already know about pirates and privateers?
prompts.W: What do you want to learn about pirates and privateers?
prompts.L: What did you learn from reading this book?
prompts.S: What do you still want to know about pirates and privateers?

L: Pirates were robbers and murderers, not the glamorous adventurers shown in movies.
Privateers were sailors hired by a government (like England) to attack enemy ships.
Pirates used weapons like cutlasses, flintlock pistols, muskets, smoke bombs, and grappling hooks.
Life on a pirate ship was dirty, dangerous, and not glamorous at all.
Blackbeard was a notorious pirate who put smoking flares in his beard to scare people.
Modern pirates use speedboats and sell stolen goods on the black market.

grammar.title: Proper Nouns
grammar.explanation: Proper nouns are the specific names of people, places, and things. They always begin with a capital letter, no matter where they appear in a sentence.
rules: Capital letters are used for names of specific people: Blackbeard, Sir Francis Drake, Henry Morgan
Capital letters are used for names of specific places: Caribbean Sea, England, Jamaica
Capital letters are used for days, months, and holidays: Monday, January, Christmas
Common nouns (general names) are NOT capitalized: pirate, ocean, ship, sailor

exercises.sentence: Blackbeard sailed the Caribbean Sea and attacked Spanish ships.
exercises.hint: Find all the proper nouns that should be capitalized.

exercises.sentence: Sir Francis Drake was a privateer for the Queen of England.

exercises.question: Which sentence capitalizes proper nouns correctly?
options: henry Morgan attacked Spanish ships in the Caribbean sea.
Henry Morgan attacked Spanish ships in the Caribbean Sea.
Henry morgan attacked spanish ships in the caribbean sea.
Henry Morgan attacked spanish ships in the Caribbean Sea.
exercises.explanation: All proper nouns must be capitalized: Henry, Morgan, Spanish, Caribbean, Sea.

exercises.question: Which word should NOT be capitalized?
exercises.explanation: "Pirate" is a common noun (general word), not a proper noun (specific name).

exercises.sentence: _____ was born in England and became one of the most feared pirates.
exercises.explanation: "Blackbeard" is a proper noun (a specific person's name) and must be capitalized.

exercises.sentence: The _____ Sea was a perfect place for pirates to launch attacks.
exercises.explanation: "Caribbean" is a proper noun (a specific place) and must be capitalized.

introduction.title: Glamorized
introduction.content: GLAMORIZED = made to seem more exciting than reality. Movies make pirates look COOL, but the truth was ugly and dangerous!

introduction.title: Author's Point of View
introduction.content: The author's purpose is to INFORM us about the REAL lives of pirates — not the movie version. Words like 'scoundrels' and 'vicious' show the author doesn't glamorize pirates.

goldenAge.title: Privateers / Launch
goldenAge.content: PRIVATEERS = sailors hired by a government to attack enemy ships. LAUNCH = to set in motion. The Queen of England LAUNCHED privateers against Spain!

goldenAge.title: Cause & Effect
goldenAge.content: Cause: Spain was getting rich from the Americas. Effect: England hired privateers to steal Spain's gold. Cause: Caribbean had small islands. Effect: Perfect hiding place for pirates!

piratesAttack.title: Cutlass / Grappling Hook / Black Market
piratesAttack.content: CUTLASS = short sword for close fighting. GRAPPLING HOOK = climbing tool to board ships. BLACK MARKET = illegal trading of stolen goods.

piratesAttack.title: Attack Sequence
piratesAttack.content: 1) Fire warning shot → 2) Hope crew surrenders → 3) Throw smoke bombs → 4) Use grappling hooks to climb aboard → 5) Fight with cutlasses and guns → 6) Take hostages and treasure

lifeOnShip.title: Helmsman / Cooper / Caulking / Rowdy
lifeOnShip.content: HELMSMAN = steers the ship. COOPER = makes/repairs barrels. CAULKING = sealing deck gaps. ROWDY = noisy and disorderly at mealtimes!

lifeOnShip.title: Captain vs Crew
lifeOnShip.content: Captain: wore silk pants, lace cuffs, gold buttons, silver buckles, long hair with ribbon. Crew: simple shoes, pants, waist sash, scarf. Captain got the BIGGEST share of treasure!

wellKnown.title: Notorious / Maidservant
wellKnown.content: NOTORIOUS = famous for something BAD. Blackbeard was a NOTORIOUS pirate. MAIDSERVANT = woman doing domestic work on ship.

wellKnown.title: Famous Pirates
wellKnown.content: BLACKBEARD: smoking flares in beard, cut fingers off for rings. SIR FRANCIS DRAKE: privateer for Queen Elizabeth, attacked ships AND mule trains. HENRY MORGAN: became lieutenant governor of Jamaica!

conclusion.title: Black Market (revisited)
conclusion.content: Modern pirates still sell stolen goods on the BLACK MARKET — just like pirates of yesterday. But they use speedboats instead of sailing ships!

conclusion.title: Then vs Now
conclusion.content: Yesterday: sailing ships, cutlasses, Caribbean. Today: speedboats, modern weapons, worldwide. BUT in movies: pirates are still GLAMORIZED as daring adventurers!
